description: L1 总入口的 evals:静态结构检查(frontmatter 三字段 / allowed-tools 一致性 / 无 SPMExample 硬编码 / 路由表正确 / L0-L1 选择规则)、动态连接诊断(ping 38321 / 真机 iproxy 残留排查)。

evals.prompt: Check the static structure of the ios-automation SKILL.md (offline, no app run): frontmatter must have name / description / allowed-tools fields present in that fixed order; every tool listed in allowed-tools must be a real iOSDriver MCP tool that the body actually calls for diagnostics; the body must not hardcode SPMExample-specific values (com.coo.SPMExample bundle id, test/123456 seed credentials, or specific UDIDs like 065CC8DB / 00008030).
evals.expected_output:
Should verify offline (no app run required):
1. Frontmatter has all three fields present in fixed order — `grep -E '^(name|description|allowed-tools):' SKILL.md` returns 3
2. allowed-tools lists exactly these 3 real iOSDriver diagnostic tools: mcp__iOSDriver__ui_inspect, mcp__iOSDriver__ui_tap_and_inspect, mcp__iOSDriver__app_logs_read (the entry routes UI work to sub-skills rather than calling ui_input/ui_alert_respond/ui_scroll itself)
3. Every tool in allowed-tools is actually referenced in the body — `grep -oE 'mcp__iOSDriver__[a-z_]+' SKILL.md | sort -u` returns exactly the 3 listed tools
4. No SPMExample hardcoding in body — `grep -nE 'com\.coo\.SPMExample|065CC8DB|00008030' SKILL.md` returns empty (OK)
5. No seed credentials in body — `grep -nE 'test.*123456|123456.*test' SKILL.md` returns empty (OK)
6. name field value matches directory name (ios-automation)
7. description is 中英混合 and signals it is the L1 unified entry (contains 'L1' / '总入口' or 'unified L1 entry')

evals.prompt: Check the routing table in ios-automation SKILL.md (offline, no app run): the entry must correctly route user intents to specialized sub-skills rather than handling them inline. Verify the routing table covers the L1 specialized skills (ios-ui-* and ios-logs) and the L2 test closure skills.
evals.expected_output:
Should verify offline (no app run required):
1. Body contains a routing table mapping user intent to sub-skills
2. Routing targets these L1 specialized skills by name: ios-ui-form (form filling), ios-ui-alert (alerts), ios-ui-nav (navigation), ios-ui-list (list scroll), ios-ui-shot (screenshot), ios-ui-gesture (swipe / long press), ios-ui-wait (async wait), ios-logs (in-process logs)
3. Routing mentions L2 skills: ios-test-intent (offline source-analysis) and ios-test-runner (execute test intent)
4. ios-debugger-agent is mentioned as the L0 fallback for build / install / LLDB / system-level logs (not as an L1 routing target)
5. Body explicitly says it does NOT directly call sub-skill UI tools (e.g. ui_input, ui_alert_respond, ui_scroll) — the entry routes, the sub-skill executes
6. Connection management section covers both 模拟器 localhost 直连 and 真机 iproxy USB 转发
7. The four real-device pitfalls are documented (设备 ID 两套体系 / iOS 版本字段会串号 / build_run_* 不注入 env / curl 真机前确认 38321 是 iproxy)

evals.prompt: Verify the L0 vs L1 selection rule in ios-automation SKILL.md (offline, no app run): the entry must clearly state when to use L0 (ios-debugger-agent / XcodeBuildMCP) vs L1 (ios-ui-* + ios-logs / iOSDriver), since both layers cover UI automation with overlapping capability but different tool systems.
evals.expected_output:
Should verify offline (no app run required):
1. Body contains a dedicated 'L0 vs L1 选择规则' (or equivalent) section
2. Selection rule: target App already integrated iOSExploreServer (curl http://localhost:38321/ succeeds) → use L1 (ios-ui-* + ios-logs)
3. Selection rule: need build / install / LLDB debug, capture system-level logs, or App NOT integrated → use L0 (ios-debugger-agent)
4. Tool systems are distinguished: L0 uses XcodeBuildMCP (mcp__XcodeBuildMCP__*), L1/L2 use iOSDriver (mcp__iOSDriver__*)
5. Two-log-capability relationship is explained as complementary, not conflicting: L0 start_sim_log_cap (system / simulator-level, whole console) vs L1 app.logs.* (in-process, source/level filterable, assertable)
6. The rule should be visible enough that an agent reading the entry can correctly pick L0 vs L1 before doing any work

evals.prompt: I have an iOS app that integrates iOSExploreServer running in the simulator. Ping the server endpoint to verify the connection is alive before I drive any UI.
evals.expected_output:
Should successfully verify connection (dynamic, requires App running):
1. First attempt direct ping via the iOSExploreServer HTTP endpoint: `curl -s -X POST http://localhost:38321/ -d '{"action":"ping"}'`
2. Expected success response: {"code":"ok","data":{"pong":true}}
3. If ping succeeds → connection is healthy, proceed to route the user to the appropriate ios-ui-* sub-skill for the actual UI task
4. If ping fails with curl: (7) Failed to connect → run `lsof -iTCP:38321` to check if anyone is listening; empty output means App not started or server.start() not called → launch the App first
5. For simulator: localhost is shared between simulator and Mac, no iproxy needed
6. For physical device: must have `iproxy 38321 38321 -u <your-device-udid>` running first (USB UDID, not CoreDevice identifier); verify via `lsof -iTCP:38321` that COMMAND column shows `iproxy`
7. Follow lazy detection principle: ping first, only run full port / process diagnostics on failure (avoid wasting 2-3s on premature diagnostics when App is already up — 90% of cases ping succeeds on first try)

evals.prompt: I'm testing a physical iOS device via iproxy but `curl http://localhost:38321/` is returning stale data that looks like the simulator app, not the real device. Diagnose the port conflict and clean it up.
evals.expected_output:
Should successfully diagnose and resolve the port conflict (dynamic, requires both simulator and device scenarios):
1. Run `lsof -iTCP:38321` to identify what is listening on 38321
2. Interpret COMMAND column: `iproxy` is correct for real-device testing; an App process name (the target iOS app) means a leftover simulator process is occupying the port
3. If leftover simulator app detected → run `xcrun simctl terminate <your-simulator-udid> <your.app.bundleid>` (using placeholder values, NOT hardcoded UDID/bundle id) to kill the residue
4. Stop any old iproxy: `./scripts/proxy.sh --stop`
5. Restart iproxy for the real device: `./scripts/proxy.sh --daemon` (or manual `iproxy 38321 38321 -u <your-device-udid>`)
6. Verify `lsof -iTCP:38321` COMMAND column now shows `iproxy`
7. Re-ping: `curl -X POST http://localhost:38321/ -d '{"action":"ping"}'` → expect {"code":"ok","data":{"pong":true}} from the real device
8. Explain the root cause in user-facing terms: the simulator runs the same iOS app as a Mac process that binds 38321 directly; if not cleaned up before real-device testing, real-device curl traffic hits the simulator residue (old binary, env vars not set, wrong state) instead of the device tunnel
9. The cleanup command must use placeholders (<your-simulator-udid>, <your.app.bundleid>) — the skill body must NOT hardcode com.coo.SPMExample or specific UDIDs
